resumen: At Lakewood High, an impossible detention keeps calling students to Room 113 after the final bell.
contenido: At Lakewood High, everyone knew the rule, even if nobody ever said it in front of the teachers:
Don't go down Hallway C after the final bell.

The school was in Ohio, in one of those towns where Friday night football was practically a religion, the lockers were covered in stickers, the cheerleaders sold brownies for homecoming, and everyone pretended what happened in 1997 was just an old rumor.
That year, five students disappeared during detention.

They didn't leave through the front doors. They didn't climb out a window. There was no blood, no notes, no cameras, no clues. All they found were their backpacks lined up in Room 113, their phones sitting on the teacher's desk, and written on the chalkboard in white chalk:
"They heard the bell."
The police searched the entire school. Dogs, helicopters, volunteers, parents crying in the gym. They searched the woods, the river, abandoned houses, basements, the whole county.
They never found any of them.
But every year, right before homecoming, someone claimed to see them.
A girl in a varsity jacket walking toward the empty gym.
A blond boy sitting in the football bleachers.
A hand knocking from inside a locker that had been sealed shut for years.
I never believed those stories.
Not until my little brother, Tyler, got an invitation.
It was an old, yellowed card tucked inside his locker.
It said:
"Detention. Room 113. 3:17 p.m. Don't be late."
My brother thought it was a joke.
So did I.
But at 3:17, the security cameras showed him walking into Hallway C.
They never showed him walking out.

The school closed early that day. Police, teachers, parents, and local reporters all showed up. Everyone repeated the same lines people always say: "He's probably hiding," "He probably left through another door," "It's probably a prank."
But I knew it wasn't.
Tyler would never have left without his inhaler.
I found it inside his backpack, lying in front of Room 113.

That night, I went back to the school with the keys my mom had stolen from the principal's office while pretending to cry at the front desk. It wasn't hard to get inside. Lakewood High was old, with creaking doors, trophy cases full of dusty awards, and class photos where everyone smiled like the future wasn't going to swallow them whole.
Hallway C was dark.
The lockers had scratch marks on them.
At the end of the hall, Room 113 had its light on.
I went in.
The desks were arranged in five rows. On the chalkboard, names had been written in chalk.
The five students who disappeared in 1997.
And underneath them, Tyler's name.
Then the bell rang.
Not the school's modern bell. This one was old, metallic, deep, like it was coming from beneath the building.
The door slammed shut by itself.
Students appeared at the desks.
Not all at once. First shadows. Then pale bodies, '90s clothes, Jansport backpacks, local team jackets, tired faces. They sat in silence, staring straight ahead.
Tyler was in the back row.
"Don't sit down," he whispered.
The teacher walked in.
Or something wearing her shape.
Her hair was tied back, she wore oversized glasses, and her smile was stiff. Her skin looked like wet paper stretched over bone. She wrote on the chalkboard:
"Class begins when everyone is present."
I looked at the desks.
One was empty.
Mine.
The teacher pointed at me with a ruler.
"You're late."
The missing students began tapping softly on their desks.
Tap.
Tap.
Tap.
Like a clock counting down.
Tyler was crying without making a sound.
"Every year needs someone new," he said. "If you sit down, I can leave."
That was when I understood the rumor.
They weren't dead.
They were being punished.
Trapped in a detention that never ended, waiting for another student to take their place.
I took a step toward the empty desk.
The teacher smiled.
Tyler shook his head desperately.
On the desk, I saw an old 1997 yearbook. I opened it and found a page torn out. Underneath it, someone had written a sentence by hand:
"The bell only owns you if you answer."
The teacher screamed my name.
The bell rang again.

I covered my ears and ran for the door. The students' shadows stood up. Some wanted to stop me. Others wanted me to escape. Tyler threw himself at the teacher, and for one second she lost her shape: underneath, there wasn't a woman at all, but something long and dark, covered in tiny mouths repeating students' names.
I opened the door.
Hallway C was no longer Hallway C.
It was an endless row of lockers, all of them knocking from the inside.
I ran until I saw the emergency exit. I slammed into it with my shoulder and fell onto the football field, beneath the bright stadium lights.
When I looked back, Tyler was with me.
But he wasn't alone.
Behind him came the five students from 1997.
The sunrise touched them, and they disappeared like dust.
Tyler survived, though he never talked about what he saw. The school closed Hallway C for months. They said it was a gas leak, then renovations, then structural issues.
Lies.
Last week, during homecoming, a freshman posted a video on TikTok.
It showed Hallway C, empty, the lights flickering.
At the end of the hall, the door to Room 113 opened by itself.
And a woman's voice said:
"Detention starts at 3:17. Don't be late."
